did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Machine Learning (specifically Decision Tree, Random Forest, Support Vector Machines, and Naive Bayes classifiers) combined with Topological Data Analysis (TDA) to classify malignant versus benign skin cancer from skin lesion images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the extraction of topological and statistical features from 800 skin lesion images and the application of four machine learning classifiers (Decision Tree, Random Forest, SVM, Naive Bayes) to classify malignant versus benign skin cancer. It reports specific performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, AUC) and compares results across different feature sets and testing sizes, which constitutes a technical study producing new results rather than a literature review.

title: Malignant Vs Benign Skin Cancer Classification Using Topological Data Analysis and Machine Learning
journal: 2025 IEEE 9th Forum on Research and Technologies for Society and Industry (RTSI)
brief_summary: This study addresses the classification of malignant versus benign skin cancer using a parameter-free approach combining Topological Data Analysis (TDA) and Machine Learning (ML). The researchers extracted six topological features and two statistical features from a dataset of 800 skin lesion images sourced from Kaggle. Four ML classifiers—Decision Tree, Random Forest, Support Vector Machines, and Naive Bayes—were evaluated using metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and AUC. The Random Forest classifier achieved the highest performance with an accuracy of 91.25%, an F1 score of 0.9125, and an AUC of 0.9678. The results indicate that using six topological features alone yielded better classification accuracy than combining them with statistical features, demonstrating the effectiveness of TDA in detecting complex structural patterns in skin lesions.

lesion_types_studied.details: 400 images of benign skin moles from a Kaggle dataset used for binary classification against malignant skin moles.
400 images of malignant skin moles from a Kaggle dataset used for binary classification against benign skin moles.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (benign vs. malignant skin cancer)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification of skin lesions into 'benign' and 'malignant' classes using machine learning classifiers (Decision Tree, Random Forest, SVM, Naive Bayes) on topological and statistical features extracted from 800 skin lesion images. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and AUC.

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Skin Cancer Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset sourced from kaggle.com containing high-resolution skin lesion images used for cancer detection and medical imaging research. The dataset consists of 800 images, with 400 images of benign skin moles and 400 images of malignant skin moles.

use.training: Used for training machine learning classifiers (Decision Tree, Random Forest, SVM, Naive Bayes) with varying testing sizes (10%, 20%, 30%, 40%) to evaluate performance.
use.testing: Used for testing the classifiers with specific split ratios (10%, 20%, 30%, 40%) to calculate accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and AUC.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of classification models by examining true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives, and to derive metrics like precision, recall, and F1-score.
To measure the ratio of correct classifications to the total number of cases.
To measure the ratio of true positives to the total number of predicted positives.
To assess how well the model detects positive cases by measuring the ratio of true positives to actual positives.
To measure the ratio of true negatives to actual negatives.
To provide a single measure combining recall and precision as their harmonic mean.
To evaluate the discriminatory power of the classifiers, with the Random Forest classifier achieving the highest AUC of 0.9678.
To assess model stability and performance by dividing the dataset into training and testing sets with varying testing sizes (10%, 20%, 30%, and 40%).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, AUC) and confusion matrices across different testing sizes and feature sets.

experiment_2_statistical_features.note: The paper reports that using 6 topological features consistently outperformed using 2 statistical features across all models. Specific metric values for the 2 statistical feature experiment are not explicitly listed in the provided text chunks, but it is noted that performance was lower than the 6 topological feature set.

experiment_8_features_6_topological_2_st.note: The paper reports that using 6 topological features consistently outperformed using 8 features (6 topological + 2 statistical). Specific metric values for the 8 feature experiment are not explicitly listed in the provided text chunks, but it is noted that performance was lower than the 6 topological feature set, with 'big differences' found in Support Vector Machine performance.

architecture_used.main_model: Random Forest (best performing), Decision Tree, Support Vector Machine (SVM), Gaussian Naive Bayes
architecture_used.input_size: null (Features extracted from 800 skin lesion images; specific image dimensions not reported)

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Topological Data Analysis (TDA) using Persistent Homology to extract 6 topological features (mean and standard deviation of homology classes of dimensions 0 and 1, persistent entropy of dimensions 0 and 1) and 2 statistical features (mean and standard deviation of pixel values). The best performance was achieved using only the 6 topological features.

classifier_details.Decision Tree: Single model splitting data based on feature values; described as resembling a flowchart.
classifier_details.Random Forest: Ensemble method; reported highest accuracy (91.25%) and AUC (0.9678) with 6 topological features.
classifier_details.Support Vector Machine: Used for binary classification; performance noted to have 'big differences' when adding statistical features to topological ones.
classifier_details.Gaussian Naive Bayes: Probabilistic classifier; reported AUC of 0.9262 (10% test) to 0.9289 (40% test) with 6 topological features.

training_details.dataset: Kaggle Skin Cancer Dataset (800 images: 400 benign, 400 malignant)
training_details.split_strategy: Varying testing sizes: 10%, 20%, 30%, and 40% (implied training sizes of 90%, 80%, 70%, 60%)
training_details.hyperparameters: null (Not explicitly reported for the ML classifiers; TDA described as 'parameter-free')

classification_or_output_head.output: Class label (Benign/Malignant) with associated probabilities/metrics (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, AUC)

preprocessing_techniques: Feature extraction using Topological Data Analysis (TDA) via Persistent Homology to compute 6 topological features (mean and standard deviation of homology classes of dimensions 0 and 1, persistent entropy of dimensions 0 and 1) and 2 statistical features (mean and standard deviation of pixel values).
conclusions: The proposed parameter-free approach combining Topological Data Analysis (TDA) and Machine Learning (ML) effectively classifies malignant versus benign skin cancers with high accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and AUC.
The Random Forest classifier achieved the highest performance among the tested ML methods, with an accuracy of 91.25%, an F1 score of 0.9125, a precision of 91.27%, and an AUC of 0.9678.

Using six topological features alone yielded consistently better classification performance compared to using only two statistical features or a combination of six topological and two statistical features.
The high uniform performance of the six topological features across all models indicates the high discriminative power of TDA in identifying complex structural patterns in skin lesions.

The addition of statistical features to topological features did not improve classification accuracy and, in some cases (particularly for Support Vector Machines), resulted in noticeable performance differences compared to using topological features alone.

The proposed method outperformed deep learning baselines (CNNs with 3 and 4 layers) in terms of accuracy, achieving higher results than the 86.2% and 87.8% maximum accuracies reported for the CNN models.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, balanced dataset of only 800 images (400 benign, 400 malignant) sourced from Kaggle, which limits the generalizability of the results to broader, more diverse clinical populations.

The paper does not report external validation; the model was only evaluated on the internal Kaggle dataset using varying train-test splits (10%, 20%, 30%, 40%), raising concerns about overfitting or dataset-specific bias.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of performance differences between classifiers or feature sets.

The comparison with deep learning baselines (CNNs with 3 and 4 layers) is methodologically weak; the CNNs were likely not optimized or trained on the same scale as the ML models, making the claim of superior performance potentially misleading.

The paper mentions that skin lesion photos are accompanied by background information used in categorization, but does not explicitly state if background removal or segmentation was performed, posing a risk of data leakage where the model learns background artifacts rather than lesion features.
Hyperparameters for the machine learning classifiers (Decision Tree, Random Forest, SVM, Naive Bayes) are not reported, preventing reproducibility of the specific model configurations.

The claim that the approach is 'parameter-free' applies only to the Topological Data Analysis (TDA) feature extraction; the subsequent machine learning classifiers themselves require parameter tuning, which is not detailed.

The dataset source (Kaggle) is not specified beyond 'open source,' lacking details on patient demographics, image acquisition devices, or ground truth verification methods (e.g., biopsy confirmation rates), which are critical for clinical relevance.
